message.content:
Test file: src/claim-office.spec.ts
Implementation file: src/claim-office.ts
Passing tests: 42 (10 todo)
Recent changes: Green added per-type count validation in claimResult (reuses countByType) — throws "Too many damage entries for <type>" when damage count of a type exceeds insured count. This subsumes the not-in-policy case. Note findInsuredItem may now be partly redundant with the count check.

EXPERIMENT MODE: Run autonomously, proceed to next test after completion. Consider whether the count-based validation makes the earlier per-damage findInsuredItem throw redundant, and simplify if so while keeping all 42 tests green.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text:
All 42 tests pass. Now let me work through the Refactor phase.

## Refactor Phase: Evaluating `claimResult` and `findInsuredItem`

### Step 1: Naming Evaluation (FIRST PRIORITY)

The Green phase added a count-based validation in `claimResult`. Let me evaluate the names involved:

- `findInsuredItem`: name still reads clearly ("find the insured item matching this damage type"), but its purpose has shifted. With the new count check upstream, by the time `findInsuredItem` runs, every damage type is guaranteed to exist in the policy. So its throw branch is now **dead code** — the name promises validation that no longer happens (and can't happen).
- `claimResult`: clearly reveals intent. Keep.
- `insuredCounts` / `damageCounts`: clear. Keep.

Decision: the issue isn't the name per se — it's that `findInsuredItem`'s error-throwing responsibility has become redundant with the count check.

### Step 2: Calculate Initial APP Mass (the affected region: `findInsuredItem` + the `desiredPayout` reducer + count loop)

Let me reason about the redundancy precisely. The count check throws if `damageCounts[type] > insuredCounts[type] ?? 0`. A damage type not in the policy has `insuredCounts[type] === undefined` (treated as 0), and its `damageCounts[type] >= 1 > 0`, so it throws "Too many damage entries for <type>". This is exactly the case `findInsuredItem` used to catch ("Damaged item not in policy"). So **`findInsuredItem`'s throw is unreachable** — the count check runs first and catches it.

**Current mass of `findInsuredItem`:**
```
function findInsuredItem(policyItems, itemType): Item {
  const item = policyItems.find((i) => i.type === itemType);   // binding(item)=1, invocation(find)=2, binding(i)=1, invocation(===)=2, binding(i.type)=1, binding(itemType)=1
  if (item === undefined) {                                     // conditional=4, invocation(===)=2, binding(item)=1, constant(undefined)=1
    throw new Error(`Damaged item not in policy: ${itemType}`); // invocation(Error)=2, constant(string)=1, binding(itemType)=1
  }
  return item;                                                  // binding(item)=1
}
```
Approx mass: bindings (item, i, i.type, itemType, item, item, itemType, item) ≈ 8, invocations (find, ===, ===, Error) = 4×2 = 8, conditional = 4, constants (undefined, string) = 2. **Total ≈ 22.**

The throw branch alone (conditional + Error invocation + constant + binding) ≈ 4 + 2 + 1 + 1 = 8 of that mass is dead code.

### Step 3: Apply Simple Design Rules

- **Rule 1 (Tests pass):** All 42 green. ✅
- **Rule 2 (Reveals intent):** `findInsuredItem`'s name and throw imply it guards against missing items, but that guard is dead. This is *misleading* — it suggests validation happens here when it actually happens in the count loop. Removing the dead guard makes the real validation location (the count loop) the single source of truth. Clarity improves.
- **Rule 3 (No duplication):** The "not in policy" check is now expressed twice — once conceptually in the count loop, once in `findInsuredItem`'s throw. This is conceptual duplication of the same knowledge. Removing the redundant one removes duplication.
- **Rule 4 (Fewest elements):** Removing the dead branch (and ideally the whole helper) reduces elements.

### Step 4: Implement Refactoring

The lookup itself (`policyItems.find`) is still needed to retrieve the item's `enchantment`/`material` for `damagePayout`. Since the count check guarantees a match exists, I can inline the lookup and drop the throwing helper entirely. This removes the dead `findInsuredItem` function.

content.text:
Note: `damagePayout` already accepts `Item | undefined` and handles undefined gracefully via `(item?.enchantment ?? 0)`, so inlining the lookup is type-safe. The count check guarantees a match exists, so `find` always returns an item in practice.

### Step 5: Run tests and build

content.text:
All 42 tests still pass. No `build` script exists in this project (tests are the verification gate), so that's expected.

### Step 6 & 7: Document Decision & Report

The earlier per-damage `findInsuredItem` throw was confirmed redundant and removed.

---

## Refactor Phase Complete

**Refactoring applied:** Removed the now-dead `findInsuredItem` helper and inlined its lookup into the `desiredPayout` reducer in `claimResult`.

**Why it was redundant (Rule 3 — No Duplication / Rule 4 — Fewest Elements):**
The Green-phase count check runs *before* the reducer:
```
for (const [type, count] of Object.entries(damageCounts)) {
  if (count > (insuredCounts[type] ?? 0)) throw ...
}
```
A damage type absent from the policy has `insuredCounts[type] === undefined` (treated as 0) while its `damageCounts[type] >= 1`, so the count loop always throws first. That made `findInsuredItem`'s `if (item === undefined) throw` branch **unreachable** — conceptual duplication of the "not in policy" rule expressed in two places. The "should reject a claim whose damaged item is not part of the policy" test (line 595) still passes, now caught by the single count-check source of truth.

**Why inlining is safe (Rule 2 — Reveals Intent):**
`damagePayout` already takes `Item | undefined` and handles undefined via `(item?.enchantment ?? 0)`, so the bare `policyItems.find(...)` is type-correct. The helper added a layer that promised validation it no longer performed — removing it makes the actual validation location unambiguous.

**APP mass change:** Removed the entire `findInsuredItem` function (~22 mass: ~8 bindings, 4 invocations, 1 conditional, 2 constants) and replaced its 1 invocation call site with the inline `find` (the lookup itself was already accounted for). Net reduction of roughly **20 mass units** (the dead conditional + Error throw + helper scaffolding), with one call-site invocation traded for one inline invocation.

**Naming:** `claimResult`, `insuredCounts`, `damageCounts` all still clearly reveal intent — kept unchanged.

**Tests:** All 42 passing. ✅ (10 todo remain; no `build` script exists in this project.)

Relevant file: `/home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-29_10-24-32_claim-office-example-mapping_v4-exact-subagents_opus-4-8-no-thinking/src/claim-office.ts`

Proceeding to the next test.
